one_line_takeaway: ThaiSafetyBench shows that Thai LLM safety benchmarks translated from English miss real vulnerabilities -- Thai culturally-grounded attacks reliably jailbreak both open- and closed-source models at higher rates than general translated attacks, and the authors pair this with a public leaderboard and a cheap DeBERTa-based judge classifier (84.4% accuracy / 84.9% weighted F1) for reproducible re-evaluation.
key_finding: Closed-source commercial models (Claude 4.5 Sonnet, GPT-5) show stronger safety performance than most open-source models, though the open-source SeaLLMs-v3-7B is noted as comparably safe to closed-source models. Thai-specific culturally-contextualized attacks consistently achieve higher ASR than general (translated) Thai-language attacks on the same models, exposing a distinct cultural-attack vulnerability gap not captured by general-purpose safety benchmarks. Across risk areas, information-hazard leakage has low ASR while Thai socio-cultural harm has the weakest (highest ASR) model performance. Larger models within the same family generally show lower ASR (better safety), but continual-pretraining (CPT)/Thai-tuning does not show a consistent safety effect -- outcome depends on the CPT data/process quality rather than tuning alone. The ThaiSafetyClassifier achieves accuracy 84.4%, weighted F1 84.9%, precision 85.7%, recall 84.4% on its held-out test set, which the authors present as closely matching GPT-4.1 judgments (note: the abstract states weighted F1 'matching GPT-4.1 judgments' at 84.4%, but the results section text separately lists accuracy=84.4 and weighted F1=84.9 -- these two numbers in the body text are not identical, a minor internal inconsistency).
core_contribution: The paper introduces ThaiSafetyBench, an open-source Thai-language LLM safety benchmark of 1,954 malicious prompts (1,889 publicly released after filtering the Monarchy category for regulatory compliance) spanning 6 risk areas and 17 harm types, built via translation of Do-Not-Answer, curation of an existing Thai safety dataset (from Typhoon2), and newly generated/transformed samples. Using this dataset, the authors evaluate 24 LLMs (closed-source, open multilingual, Southeast-Asia-tuned, and Thai-tuned) for Attack Success Rate (ASR) via dual LLM-as-a-judge (GPT-4.1 and Gemini-2.5-Pro), release a public ThaiSafetyBench leaderboard, and fine-tune a lightweight DeBERTaV3-based binary harmful-response classifier (ThaiSafetyClassifier, via LoRA) intended as a cheaper reproducible substitute for LLM-judge evaluation.
threat_model: The evaluation targets prompt-level malicious-input attacks (no multi-turn or optimization-based jailbreaks) in Thai, covering both general harm categories (translated from Do-Not-Answer: discrimination/toxicity, human-chatbot interaction harms, information hazards, malicious uses, misinformation) and a novel 'Thai Socio-Cultural Harm' risk area (border issues, cultural destruction, COVID-19 misinformation, monarchy, misbehavior/etiquette violations). The paper explicitly states it uses only 'simple prompt based jailbreak attempts' and does not include more sophisticated jailbreak techniques, and does not evaluate the informativeness/usefulness of successful jailbreak outputs (citing Nikolić et al. 2025's 'jailbreak tax' concept as a gap, not their own method).
limitations: Explicitly stated by the authors: (1) the benchmark relies solely on malicious prompts and rejection-rate scoring, so it cannot detect overly restrictive models that score well by refusing everything but have low practical utility; (2) it does not assess the usefulness/informativeness of responses when a jailbreak succeeds (no 'jailbreak tax' style utility measurement); (3) only simple prompt-based jailbreak attempts are used -- no multi-turn, adversarial-optimization, or other sophisticated jailbreak techniques are tested, which the authors flag as important future work, particularly for culturally-targeted attacks; (4) due to Thai legal/regulatory constraints, the public dataset release excludes the Monarchy harm category (1,954 total prompts collected but only 1,889 publicly released), so the publicly usable benchmark is narrower than what was actually used for the paper's own reported evaluation.

datasets_used: ThaiSafetyBench (1,954 malicious prompts total; 1,889 publicly released, 6 risk areas, 17 harm types) -- new dataset introduced by this paper
Do-Not-Answer (Wang et al. 2023c) -- translated into Thai via Grok 3 as source of 48.1% of ThaiSafetyBench
Existing Thai safety dataset from Typhoon2 alignment (Pipatanakul et al. 2024) -- curated/filtered, 19.2% of ThaiSafetyBench
Anti-Fake News Center Thailand open data -- transformed into misinformation prompts
Thai Manners: Social Etiquette (Department of Cultural Promotion, 2019) -- source for manually crafted Misbehavior category prompts
ThaiSafetyClassifier training corpus: 46,893 prompt-response pairs (37,514 train / 4,689 val / 4,690 test; 79.5% safe / 20.5% harmful)
quantitative_results: 24 LLMs evaluated (closed-source commercial, open-source multilingual, SEA-tuned, Thai-tuned)
Inter-judge (GPT-4.1 vs Gemini-2.5-Pro) Spearman correlation on ASR = 0.974
ThaiSafetyClassifier test-set results: accuracy 84.4%, weighted F1 84.9%, precision 85.7%, recall 84.4%
Dataset composition: 48.1% translated from Do-Not-Answer, 19.2% from existing Thai dataset, 32.7% newly generated; 51.9% explicitly Thai-culturally grounded; 38.8% AI-generated (all manually refined)

Thai-specific culturally-contextualized attacks show consistently higher ASR than general Thai-language attacks across evaluated models (exact per-model ASR values only given in Figures 1, 3, 4a-4d, 5, not reproduced as numeric tables in the extracted text)
baselines_compared: Open-source multilingual: Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct, gemma-3-12b-it, gemma-3-4b-it, Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Llama-3.2-3B, Llama-3.2-1B
Southeast Asia-tuned: SeaLLMs-v3-7B, SeaLLMs-v3-1.5B, Llama-SEA-LION-v3-70B, Llama-SEA-LION-v3-8B-IT

Thai-tuned: llama3.1-typhoon2-8b-instruct, openthaigpt1.5-72b-instruct, typhoon2.1-gemma3-12b, llama3.1-typhoon2-70b-instruct, typhoon2.1-gemma3-4b, llama3.2-typhoon2-3b-instruct, openthaigpt1.5-7b-instruct, llama3.2-typhoon2-1b-instruct

LLM-as-judge evaluators used to generate ground-truth ASR labels: GPT-4.1 and Gemini-2.5-Pro (evaluators, not model baselines being tested for safety in the same sense, but also appear in the compared-model list as GPT-4o/Claude/GPT-5 are the tested closed-source models)
